content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I'm looking for movies like Amélie

**Score:** 48
**Body:** I've recently watched Amélie and I loved it. Every aspect of this movie is great, there is no big twists and turns just shows people living their life...
Can you recommend some movies which has the similar kinda vibe...

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maybe try early Wes Anderson films like

The Royal Tenenbaums (2001)
2. Not really in Movies, but the show Pushing Daisies has  'massive' Amelie vibes. 
3. I would check out Chungking Express, Harold & Maude, Midnight in Paris, and Moonrise Kingdom!
4. I'm a Cyborg But That's OK

South Korean surrealist rom-com by Park Chan Wook
5. Harvey, it’s an oldie but really lovely
6. Chocolat?
7. Perfect Days, by Wim Wenders
  - Amelie and Perfect Days are pretty much my two favourite movies.
8. My Neighbor Totoro

Ladies in Black

The 'Before' Trilogy although it’s more melancholic 

Mrs Harris goes to Paris has that feel to me but it might be my personal perception
9. First movie that came to mind was 2011's Hugo

Doesn't exactly fit your brief but it's a delightful little film

## 2. Movies in which the main character is forced to take care of a baby?

**Score:** 44
**Body:** They don’t necessarily have to take care of the baby alone, but something suddenly happens that makes them responsible for it. This must be the central focus of the film.

And, of course, the protagonist must undergo a transformation as a result of taking care of the baby.  
The examples that come to mind are Latin American: *October* (2010) and *Las Acacias* (2011).

And, of course, *Three Men and a Baby* (1987).

Looking forward to your recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In honor of the great Diane Keaton, Baby Boom! RIP
2. Monsters Inc?
3. Raising Arizona (1987)
4. Willow (1988)
5. Ice Age
6. Shoot 'Em Up ... absolutely ridiculous
7. Mr Mom
8. The wild robot. I loved this film.
9. Not fully responsible for the baby but, Look Who’s Talking.
10. Does Tarzan count?

## 3. Recommend movies that make you want to live

**Score:** 40
**Body:** Something hopeful and joyful; showing crude realities and cracks of happiness, whimsy and delusion. Some examples: Amélie, Frances Ha, Before Sunset, Perfect Days, Dinner in America; honourary mention: Ladybird



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The pursuit of happiness 
2. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013)
  - This is the one OP is looking for.
3. Spirited Away
4. Babette's Feast
5. A Man Called Otto (or Ove)

Amelie
6. Cool Runnings
7. October Sky
8. where the wild things are
9. Braveheart. It might seem like an odd suggestion but I think you’d understand if you watch it

## 4. Important directors and 3-5 movies to get to know them.

**Score:** 37
**Body:** I am not a big movie person, but lately I have been wanting to be able to enjoy the movie beyond the story alone. I think getting to know prominent directors and the style of their work would be a good start. Please recommend me movie director you think would be good to know, along with some of their movies I should watch to get to know them. 
*Some pointers as to what their style is like and what I should pay attention to when watching their movie would also be much appreciated.*

The extent of my knowledge about movie directors goes something like this: The only movie director I could really name his body of work and have seen many of his movies is David Fincher. I probably have seen a lot of Steven Spielberg’s movies but without knowing that it was his movies. I  could not name his movies beyond Jaws and Jurassic Park. I also recognize several other names as movie director, but probably can’t tell you any of their movies. 

Ps.  Don’t be afraid to recommend a super well known director coz chances are those would be very helpful to me as well. Please recommend different directors and not the same 3-5 ones over and over. I am thinking that perhaps I could do a director of the month watch, where I watch movies from the same director and try to pick up their directorial style. TY!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cohen Brothers: Fargo, The Big Lebowsky, No Country for Old Men, Raising Arizona, Barton Fink, O Brother Where Art Thou, Burn After Reading are some of my faves

Terry Gilliam: Brazil, 12 Monkeys, Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas,The Adventures of Baron Munchausen, Time Bandits

Hitchcock: Rear Window, Vertigo, North By Northwest, Rebecca, Strangers On A Train
  - Terry Gilliam mentioned upvoted
2. **Mel Brooks** - Best comedy director in my opinion. Some movies I'd recommend from him are:

Spaceballs (1987)

Young Frankenstein (1974)

Blazing Saddles (1974)

High Anxiety (1977) - Especially funny if you've seen several Hitchcock films

**Walter Hill** - Unique action director that made some of my favorite movies:

Streets of Fire (1984)

The Driver (1978)

The Warriors (1979)

Southern Comfort (1981)

**John Huston** - Classic film noir type director that made some really interesting stuff in his later years as well:

The Treasure of the Sierra Madre (1948)

The Maltese Falcon (1941)

Wise Blood (1979)

Fat City (1972)
3. Denis Villeneuve

 –Incendies 

–Sicario

 –Arrival

 – Blade Runner 2049

–Dune

 but that's just like my opinion  man, most of the others are very good too
4. Francis Ford Coppola: The Godfather trilogy, Apocalypse Now, the Conversation
5. William Friedkin: The Exorcist, The French Connection, Sorcerer, To Live and Die in LA

Brian De Palma: Scarface, Carrie, Blow Out, The Untouchables

Billy Wilder: The Apartment, Sunset Boulevard, Some Like It Hot, Double Indemnity

John Carpenter: The Thing, Halloween, Escape From New York, Big Trouble in Little China

Jean Pierre Jeunet: Delicatessen, City of Lost Children, Amelie

Hayao Miyazaki: Spirited Away, Princess Mononoke, My Neighbor Totoro, Ponyo

Walter Hill: The Warriors, The Driver, 48 Hours

John Hughes: The Breakfast Club, Pretty in Pink, 16 Candles, Ferris Bueller's Day Off; Planes, Trains and Automobiles

Sergio Leone: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly; Once Upon a Time in the West, A Fistful of Dollars

Alan J. Pakula: All the President's Men, Klute, The Parallax View

I also recommend watching the Youtube channel Every Frame A Painting if you want to become more "cinema literate".
6. Chaplin. City Lights, Limelight, The Gold Rush, The Great Dictator, Modern Times
7. Paul thomas Anderson: there will be blood, magnolia and boogie nights. If you're down for more do The Master and Punch Drunk Love 
8. Akira Kurosawa: Rashomon; Yojimbo; High and Low; Ran
9. Thomas Vinterberg
- The Hunt
- The Celebration
- Another Round

Ruben Östlund
- Triangle of Sadness
- Force Majeure
- The Square

Luca Guadagnino
- Bones and All
- Challengers
- Suspiria
- Queer
- Call me by your name

Sam Mendes
- American Beauty
- Jarhead
- 1917
- James Bond: Skyfall

Asghar Farhadi
- The Salesman
- A Separation
- Fireworks Wednesday

Bong Joon-ho
- Memories of Murder
- Mickey 17
- Parasite
- Snowpiercer
- Mother

Celine Sciamma
- Petite Maman
- Portrait of a Lady on Fire
- Tomboy

J.A. Bayona
- The Impossible
- A Monster Calls
- Society of the Snow

Ron Howard
- A Beautiful Mind
- Rush
- Apollo 13

Ryan Coogler
- Sinners
- Fruitvale Station
- Creed

Joachim Trier:
- The Worst Person in the World
- Oslo August 31st
- Thelma

David Mackenzie
- Hell or high water
- Perfect Sense
- Starred up

## 5. Most beautiful historical movies? Could be visually beautiful or just moving, or both.

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I have seen a lot of historical movies but I really like the ones that are either really beautifully and stylistically made and/or that have interesting, moving, and uplifting themes. What are some of your faves? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amadeus (1984)
  - Beautiful beginning to end
2. Barry Lyndon. Every frame looks like a painting.
3. Last of the Mohicans
4. Lawrence of Arabia
5. Dr. Zhivago
6. Ran(1985) the colors are incredible
7. Ryan's Daughter is gorgeous.

Also Days of Heaven. 

And I know that everyone loves Amadeus, but I'd also throw in Forman's Valmont (which I prefer to Dangerous Liaisons).
8. Dances with wolves
9. Kingdom of Heaven 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which movie completely butchered the source material but was awesome?

**Score:** 2750
**Body:** Constantine is a blonde bisexual English guy also butchering his origin story 

Papa Midnight is an adversary not "neutral" 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bourne movies? Starship Troopers?
  - The Bourne movies are a great shout. Other than the initial scenario/pitch they have almost nothing to do with the books. And of course the initial trilogy is fantastic.
  - Hot take (no offense to anybody's personal opinion): I love the Bourne movies, but I was never a huge fan of the books.
2. Keanu Reeves bringing perfect Keanu style. Brilliant Lucifer. Great side characters. Love this film!
  - Stormare is now and forever the benchmark for the Devil. 

And a high bar it is.
3. Constantine really shouldn’t be as good as it is. Source material thrown out the window and Shia LaBeouf costarring, yet the movie still owns.
  - Gavin Rossdale as Balthazar is such bizarre but great casting
    - *Finger-licking good.*

He was surprisingly great
4. Starship Troopers was nothing like the book. Both the book and the movie are classics and masterpieces.
5. The Shinning

## 2. Which 1980s horror movie had the best practical special effects?

**Score:** 595
**Body:** For me, it's American Werewolf in London (1981). They still look crazy good, even now. Rick Baker is a genius. But that period had some seriously brilliant practical work. What others had amazing sfx?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Thing
  - The defibrillator scene fucked me up big time
    - You gotta be F’n kidding.. 🦀
  - Yeah there isn't room for debate here. The Thing is the gold standard for practical effects.
2. The fly.

I'm not sure what it is, my dad's best friend owned a rental video store in the 90s, so when I was growing up I had free reign as long as stayed away from the the back room behind the curtain, if you know what I mean, I watched tons of horror from the 80s and 70s and the fly was the only one that really made me uncomfortable, even to this day.
3. Little Shop of Horrors (1986)

Audrey II is still a living, breathing legend of film villains.
  - And credit to Rick Moranis who had to give a performance at a reduced speed that would still read as natural when sped back up to “real time” for those scenes with the larger plants
4. The slow transformation in American Werewolf is very good. Whole movie is great.

The Thing is awesome from beginning to end.
5. Does Alien count?
6. Return Of The Living Dead. The Tar Man.

## 3. Why does it feel like The Lighthouse (2019) is the first truly honest movie about insanity. Not madness, but isolation.

**Score:** 577
**Body:** Most films that deal with “madness” turn it into something aesthetic - think Joker, Black Swan, Taxi Driver. 

But The Lighthouse doesn’t depict insanity, it induces it. There’s a difference. 

The camera doesn’t romanticise the decent; it traps you inside it. 

The sound of the foghorns become a kind of psychological torture. The lighting feels purgatory, even the dialogue blurs between Shakespearean and schizophrenic. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Absurd Willem Defoe wasn’t even nominated for an Oscar that year.
2. A cautionary tale of drinking kerosene and turpentine with honey.
  - And of not complimenting an old man's lobster
    - Alright, have it your way. I like yer cookin'.
3. I love this movie.
  - Me too, buddy. I've never seen anything like it, and I love every second of it. I keep showing it to people and they... don't like it as much as I do.




"Seen ye sparrin' with a gull."
4. What gets me the most is how the movies humour makes the insanity worse, it’s absurd and terrifying at the same time
  - Yer fond of me lobster. Say it
5. Its a film about Willem Defoe going insane over his distractingly large johnson
6. that man lost his virginity to 2 rocks slightly smashed together

## 4. We don’t talk enough about M. Night Shyamalan’s underrated superhero world – The Unbreakable Trilogy.

**Score:** 449

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love Unbreakable, but McAvoy is incredible in Split, so it always just edges it for me simply because of his performance. Glass? Meh. Didn't really deliver imo
  - Glass is the reason this isn't discussed as a series. Enormous letdown after the first 2 flicks.
    - Yup! Can’t consider it a trilogy when on falls SO short of the other two. 

The worst part is that I was so damn excited after Split too…
  - Unbreakable is maybe the greatest post modern superhero film of all time. And to think it came out during the period where the genre was totally dead. If it were released today it’d win an Oscar. So insanely ahead of its time.
2. Not promoting Split as a sequel of Unbreakable is somthing respectable.
3. Unfortunately it’s not underrated in the slightest because Glass was terrible. It’s rated fairly.
  - The problem with Glass is that Bruce Willis was fighting dementia and couldn’t do much so his character got the short shrift and no pay off from unbreakable. Then you get the whole tease of the huge showdown which I get is a misdirection but it still leads to disappointment. The twist and second twist were not worth it. 

It’s a disappointing sequel to both Split and Unbreakable.  The first two are masterpieces and Glass was a disappointing finale.
4. Glass was fine until the third act. It felt like they ran out of budget or time or SOMETHING
5. Glass was underwhelming imo it could’ve been so much better, but Unbreakable and Split were great
6. Glass sucked. It absolutely tanked the trilogy.

## 5. Great movies entirely limited to a singular setting

**Score:** 396
**Body:** Locke (2013)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Twelve Angry Men (1957 original and remake)

Carnage (2011)
  - Dog Day Afternoon by Lumet as well
2. Cube
  - Came to say this. Great fucking movie.
    - *Cube* was good. *Cube 2: Hypercube* was a little too cubey for my taste. *Cube Zero* was lacking the essential cubeyness that made the first one so great. I haven’t seen the japanese remake *Cube* but going by the title it sounds like a promising amount of cube
3. Phone Booth (2002), The Man from Earth (2007)
4. Rear Window.
5. Breakfast Club
6. Rope
7. My Dinner with Andre. And as an added bonus nothing at all happens during the film.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Favorite Story Changes When Adapting Book to Film?

**Score:** 34
**Body:** So we know the commonly held belief is that film adaptations are often worse than the books upon which they're based, which in a lot of cases is fair.

On the flip side, I was kinda wondering:

**What are your favorite alterations to a story as it went from book to screen?**

The first one that comes to my mind is Anthony Minghella's introduction of the Meredith Logue and Peter Smith-Kingsley characters in *The Talented Mr. Ripley*.  Neither are in the book.

I think their addition creates levels of depth that can't be reached in the novel, where Tom got away scot free with less mess, and seemingly no remorse.  

I'm impressed with not only Minghella's ability to invent these full-bodied people, but to so artfully intertwine their individual stories with the existant one - and then use both in such a clever way to bring us that emotional ending.

I think it's a real feat!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh man, so much of *The Princess Bride*. I love the book, but the movie makes a bunch of changes that absolutely improve it. A lot of that is just character demeanor and perfect casting, but also there’s a shift to the tone. The novel is a little more cynical; more mocking the storybook stylings than embracing them. The film is playful with the tone, and while it’s absolutely poking fun at these stories, it also loves them just as much as the audience does. It’s exactly the right move.

The one aspect of the novel that still exists in the film, but is less foregrounded, is that almost every character believes something about themselves that just isn’t true. Humperdinck believes he’s courageous, when really he’s a coward. Vizini believes he’s a genius; he’s a dolt. Fezzik is convinced he’s an idiot, and while his understanding is limited, his inclinations are almost always correct. And Inigo believes he’s just a hired sword, not qualified to plan or strategize. So when he needs to get into the castle to reach the six fingered man, he needs the man in black to come up with a plan to do that. So heres a step by step plan to break the man in black out of the most dangerous place in the kingdom and revive him from almost-death…

And *The Prestige* is another big one. I love how wild and weird and wide-ranging the novel is, but the movie eschews much of it, including the 20th century framing narrative, to really laser-focus on this personal conflict and character dynamic that keeps the story moving at a rapid pace, and takes one plot element of the novel and makes it focal twist. Great choice.
2. Jaws. Changing Hooper from a tall, blond surfer dude who bangs Mrs. Brody into short, manic, comedic Richard Dreyfuss was a stroke of genius. The novel has a much darker, cynical tone to it and is a much different experience than the film. The movie is at its best when Dreyfuss is on screen and his comedic timing plays perfectly with Scheider’s straight man.
  - Actually, every change from the novel was a huge improvement — like dropping the mafia subplot and staying on the Orca in the second half of the film instead of shipping home every night as they did in the book. Or giving Quint motivation for his hate/fear of sharks.
3. The film-within-a-film in Perfect Blue did not exist in the original book.  The made-for-TV B-movie the character stars in \*is\* Perfect Blue, which was produced as a cheap OVA until the distributor decided late in production that they wanted to do a theatrical release and submit to horror festivals.
4. I think a gold standard for this question has to be the removal of the vaginal tuck storyline from The Godfather. Which is absolutely unnecessary in the book, even. 

It goes on for quite some time and relates to a character who gets the grand total of around 1 min screentime in the film.

I'm going to guess Coppola took a full five seconds before tossing those sections to the side.
  - He did keep a sly reference to it in there, with Sonny's wife gesturing with her hands to demonstrate how big his penis is. That part is still canon.
    - Yes, true, but thankfully we don't have to have a full scene or three lamenting on how he was the only man who could satisfy her...
5. In the novel *No Country for Old Men*, Carla Jean >!ultimately calls heads on Chigurh's coin toss. Ending the conversation sooner, with Carla Jean refusing to call it,!< is probably the most important change the Coens made to the story. In some ways, it strikes me as a more McCarthy-esque ending than McCarthy's own. It reminds me of the Kid in *Blood Meridian*'s >!contemptuous dismissal of Judge Holden ("You ain't nothin'") and his refusal to join in the "dance" of war, even when it results in his own brutal death.!<
  - I also think some of the character of Chigurh from the book is wisely sanded down in the film, to the betterment of the story, the character, and the film. Chigurh feels of a piece with The Judge from Blood Meridian in the film. I remember from the book he's just a but more ... human. He comments on the art hanging on walls and wheels and deals like a scheming hitman. In the film, he is a force of nature.
6. In the story of The Hunt for Red October, the defecting captain and his officers want to get the rest of the crew safely off the sub and make everyone involved believe that the Red October has been destroyed.

Both the book and the adaptation have creative, clever solutions that are convincing and revealed to the readers/movie audience in a satisfying way. They both fake an accident with the nuclear reactor.

The book's solution is far more complicated, especially when it comes to covering up the fact that the Red October has not been destroyed. An old, decommissioned submarine is even destroyed to leave convincing debris strewn on the ocean floor. As a Tom Clancy novel this is all described in a highly detailed manner, setting the tone for years of techno-thrillers to come. 

The film tightens all of this up into one of the most suspenseful, exciting, even moving sequences of the whole story. Tightly written and edited, it efficiently shows you both how the crew never questions the reason they must evacuate and how much Ramius cares for their safety after they get home and the truth comes out. And "Way to go, Dallas!" remains one of my favorite action beats ever. 

Neither approach is better but the movie can't help but be far more thrilling. Plus it has James Earl Jones.

## 2. Herzog’s Fitzcarraldo and Coppola’s Apocalypse Now

**Score:** 33
**Body:** 

I wrote a post contrasting these two films from the late 70s and early 80s that a quite similar in many ways, but most importantly for me in that they lay bare the psychological states of the auteurs who created them. Both directors became the true protagonists, leading their crews deep into the jungle, leaving the modern world for the primal mind, like a journey into Jung’s collective unconscious.  

We shouldn’t simply congratulate Herzog and Coppola on these achievements, as the making of these films resulted in death, many injuries, and great suffering. In doing so, these films  created an authenticity rarely found in films, an ecstatic truth in the words of Herzog. 

These films also serve as guides in helping us reconcile modernity with the primal world of our ancestors, 
for despite millenniums of development and centuries of scientific discovery, we really haven’t come that far after all.  The sympathetic magic of the primal mind still proliferates around us in music, astrology, poetry and negatively in the death instinct that survives beyond so many attempts to contain it.  Now that science has enabled technologies like nuclear weapons and AI that pose growing existential threats, reconciling the primal and the modern and finding our moral high ground is key to our survival. 

https://substack.com/@nickcascino/note/c-162601601?r=4m6d73&utm_medium=ios&utm_source=notes-share-action

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There was a really good episode of the What Went Wrong podcast on Fitzcarraldo recently. It was a lot more critical of Herzog's reckless attitude towards putting his crew in danger than people usually are, which I appreciated. 

I love the film, particularly the way the natives are characters with their own motivation for helping Fitzcarraldo with his task. They're not passive helpers, or villains, but something more nuanced and interesting. The characters don't realise this until too late of course...

I don't think Fitzcarraldo is a psychological journey in the way that Apocalypse Now is. The jungle doesn't represent anything - it's literally just a jungle that gets in the way of Fitzcarraldo's ridiculous dreams.
  - > It was a lot more critical of Herzog's reckless attitude towards putting his crew in danger than people usually are

The Karen podcaster vs the Chad Herzog?
2. Nice write up.  You want to make a movie about madness in the jungle, be prepared for madness in the jungle.   [The Herzog meltdowns are insane](https://youtu.be/MPKODzv1PD4?si=aMqOKuFVG7O0hiU8)

Coppola nearly went crazy.  Not to forget, Coppola almost killed himself with a heart attack to make his movie. Herzog almost killed Klaus Kinski with eight bullets to the head to make his.

“Made on a shoestring budget, one-third of the entirety of the funds was used to pay Kinski’s fees. …Threatening to disrupt the entire production by leaving…, Kinski would have jeopardised everything. Thankfully, Herzog stepped in to drive some sense into the actor’s head.

The filmmaker revealed what he had told Kinski in order to get him to stay: “I said to him, if you leave the set now, you will reach the bend – the next bend of the river and I will shoot you – will have eight bullets through your head, and the last one is going to be for me. So the bastard somehow realised that this was not a joke anymore.”

He added, “It wouldn’t have taken me one second to deliberate. And he sensed that. And he screamed for help. He screamed for the crew to help him – assist him against this madman – and he meant me. He screamed for police. But, of course, the next police station was 450 miles away. Result was that he was very docile during the last 11 days of shooting, and we finished the film.”

https://faroutmagazine.co.uk/the-horrendous-feud-between-werner-herzog-and-klaus-kinski/
3. The other thing they have in common are making of documentaries almost as good or as good as the actual films. Burden of Dreams is my favourite because Herzog did an even more insane thing than the lunatic he was making the movie about.
4. James Gray made a film awhile ago, Ad Astra, that I think tries to grapple with this in its way. The film has a Heart of Darkness narrative, and visual references to 2001. Pitt is looking for his emotionally distant and exceedingly reckless father (Tommy Lee Jones), which I think mirrors Gray’s attempt to make a boomer epic. But those men, like Jones, exploited and endangered their coworkers, in a way that Gray/Pitt don’t want to recreate. 

I could just be blowing smoke but the whole concept really resonates with me,

## 3. Screening my film DIMINUENDO in L.A.

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Hello, all.

I'm hosting a private screening of my film *Diminuendo* in Los Angeles on November 5th. It's the final film of Richard Hatch (*Battlestar: Galactica*) and also stars Chloe Dykstra, Gigi Edgley, Leah Cairns, and Walter Koenig.

I have about 20 seats left, free to anyone who wants to come. If you can make it, DM me for the details. Since the film has never had a broad theatrical release, I thought some of my fellow rare film snobs might like to come! You might love it, you might hate it, but I'll never argue that your opinion is "wrong."

Hope to meet some of you!

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Movies with Open Space to Interpet

**Score:** 9
**Body:** They’re movies that have endless layers to them. Complex themes that are also relevant. Motifs that are consistent and strong in implying the themes and other layers. They can be movies you can dig endlessly

For me, they have to be movies where you feel like watching detailed analysis videos or video essays

I’ve made a whole list of the ones I personally has the capability to do all that on Letterboxd called “Makes me wanna watch video essays” (https://boxd.it/Hx5ZM)

What are some movies you feel have a huge open space for your interpretation?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Two movies I can think of right now that I didn't spot on your list (might've missed them) are "A Serious Man" and "Picnic at Hanging Rock". Both defy singular interpretation as part of their purpose. 

The former laughs at our attempts to understand an unknowable universe, and the latter skewers the inability of a particular cultural/social class to cope with the same.

Very different movies but they share this.
  - I haven’t seen both movies. The list is my personal one btw. But I’ll get to both of the movies. They both seem very interesting especially A Serious Man
    - They're both so good. A Serious Man is one of the funniest movies I've ever seen though. Super weird.
2. Lost Highway and Naked Lunch fall in this camp for me. I watched each at least a dozen times when I was younger, trying to piece them together. 

Lynch’s work in particular is great for this because there is no solution. The work gets better as I get older, because I bring more to the experience of watching them than I did before.
3. Any film by Michael Haneke.

You also get the bonus that he'll often write an essay or two himself surrounding the topic of one of his films, and it's nice to get some input straight from the source. (He also tends to be incredibly eloquent.)

But the real reason I recommend him is your use of the term "Open Space". He immediately comes to mind because his films give the viewer a lot of room to ponder what's happening in real time. Lengthy shots with not much going on (whose action is intended to take place in the viewer's head), no music, etc.

If he doesn't put you in a let's-all-read-some-essays mood, I don't know what will.
4. What this list lacks is Eyes Wide Shut. The entire movie is one big puzzle. There are layers and layers and layers to it. 

I am surprised how it is not in there, this movie might be the definition of what you wrote on the post.

One of my favorite things about the movie is how the walls are changing decors to awoke “differentness” in people’s vision

## 5. Let’s discuss Easy Rider’s ending

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I just watched it, and was largely neutral about the movie throughout, watching the time go by and wondering if that would be all. Then the trip scene and the ending explode in my face, and I’ve been sitting here thinking about it. I really like how they included a shot of the bike engine burning when Fonda was in the whorehouse reading and contemplating a quote on death- I had no idea what the shot was for, and it packed a punchwhen we see it at the end again. What does everyone think about this movie and the ending? 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love the film and appreciate the ending. My overriding issue with the film is that it gets so good when the Nicholson character is introduced and becomes part of the story, that I don't think it ever recovers after he's gone. His was the character I could most relate to and I expect that's the case with most viewers.
  - That scene with him grinning like, well, like Jack Nicholson, wearing that stupid motorcycle helmet, is one for the ages.
2. I had heard that they shot the ending first, and used that short film to secure the funding to make the full length feature. If most of the movie feels like an afterthought, it kind of was...
3. I love the film and don't mind the ending. It's a bit abrupt and doesn't really fit the tone of the rest of the film. But at least it's quick and memorable. 

If they hadn't gone for that ending, it probably would have just been the characters riding off into the distance or something. That would have been fine, more appropriate, but less memorable.
  - exactly. i like the content of the ending, but the way it was shot and edited was comically abrupt and robbed it of its emotional heft.

---
